title: ConsistBench: A Cross-Format, Cross-Phrasing Consistency Benchmark for Large Language Models
description: We propose ConsistBench, a benchmark of ~1,000 base questions expanded into ~8,000-10,000 evaluation instances across 5 answer formats (MCQ, open-ended, yes/no, true/false, fill-in-the-blank) and 6 paraphrase types (lexical, syntactic, voice, formality, negation-framing, elaborative). Unlike OSQ-bench which measures aggregate accuracy gaps between MCQ and open-ended formats, ConsistBench evaluates item-level cross-format agreement across 5 formats jointly with paraphrase-type-stratified consistency, enabling fine-grained diagnosis of when and why LLMs give inconsistent answers to semantically equivalent inputs.
motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications requiring reliability, yet they exhibit alarming inconsistency when the same question is rephrased or presented in a different format. OSQ-bench (Myrzakhan et al., 2024) demonstrated a 25% average accuracy drop from MCQ to open-ended format across 40+ LLMs, but measures only aggregate accuracy — not per-question consistency. RoParQ (Choi, 2025) tests paraphrase consistency but only in MCQ format with a single aggregate metric. ParaRel (Elazar et al., 2021) is limited to masked LMs. No benchmark jointly evaluates cross-format consistency (with item-level metrics across 5 formats) and paraphrase-type-stratified consistency, preventing the community from diagnosing which formats, paraphrase types, and domains expose the worst inconsistency.
proposed_approach: 1) Curate ~1,000 base questions from 6 knowledge domains (science, history, math, commonsense, world knowledge, logic) using established open QA datasets (~165 per domain). 2) For each question, generate 5 format variants (MCQ, open-ended, yes/no, true/false, fill-in-the-blank) programmatically with 10% manual verification. 3) For a 300-question subset, generate paraphrase variants across 6 controlled types using LLM generation with validation and 10% manual spot-checking. 4) Evaluate 6-7 LLMs (3.8B to 70B quantized) on all ~8,000-10,000 instances. 5) Use two-stage answer equivalence: rule-based normalization + LLM-based semantic judge validated against ~200 human-annotated pairs. 6) Compute novel consistency metrics: Cross-Format Agreement (CFA), Cross-Phrasing Agreement (CPA) per paraphrase type, Format-Conditional Accuracy Gap (FCAG), Consistency-Accuracy Ratio (CAR), and Paraphrase Fragility Index (PFI). 7) Analyze consistency across domains, model sizes (3.8B-70B), and model families.

related_work.title: Measuring and Improving Consistency in Pretrained Language Models
related_work.authors: Elazar, Kassner, Ravfogel, Ravichander, Hovy, Schütze, Goldberg
related_work.relevance: Introduced ParaRel for paraphrase consistency evaluation but limited to masked LMs and cloze-style probing. We extend to generative LLMs with cross-format evaluation and paraphrase typology.

related_work.title: Open-LLM-Leaderboard: From Multi-choice to Open-style Questions for LLMs Evaluation, Benchmark, and Arena
related_work.relevance: Most directly related prior work. Converts 8 MCQ benchmarks to open-style questions and evaluates 40+ LLMs, finding 25% average accuracy drop. However, OSQ-bench measures only aggregate accuracy gaps, not item-level consistency; tests only 2 formats (MCQ vs. open); and does not evaluate paraphrase robustness. ConsistBench measures per-question agreement across 5 formats with paraphrase-type stratification.

related_work.title: BeHonest: Benchmarking Honesty of Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Chern, Hu, Yang, Chern, Guo, Jin, Wang, Liu
related_work.relevance: Evaluates honesty including some consistency scenarios (format perturbation, social pressure), but does not test systematic paraphrase consistency or cross-format consistency.

related_work.title: State of What Art? A Call for Multi-Prompt LLM Evaluation
related_work.authors: Mizrahi, Kaplan, Malkin, Dror, Shahaf, Stanovsky
related_work.relevance: Demonstrated instruction template sensitivity across 6.5M instances and 20 LLMs. Focuses on evaluation methodology rather than building a dedicated consistency benchmark.

related_work.title: RoParQ: Paraphrase-Aware Alignment of LLMs Towards Robustness to Paraphrased Questions
related_work.relevance: Tests paraphrase consistency in MCQ format with XParaCon metric. We differ by adding cross-format evaluation, paraphrase type stratification, and domain analysis.

related_work.title: Consistency in Language Models: Current Landscape, Challenges, and Future Directions
related_work.venue: ICML 2025 Workshop on Reliable and Responsible Foundation Models
related_work.relevance: Comprehensive survey identifying lack of standardized definitions and metrics for consistency. Our benchmark directly addresses this gap.

related_work.title: On Robustness and Reliability of Benchmark-Based Evaluation of Large Language Models
related_work.relevance: Studies paraphrasing effects on benchmark scores across 34 LLMs. Focuses on evaluation reliability rather than building a dedicated consistency benchmark with novel metrics.

related_work.title: PromptBench: A Unified Library for Evaluation of Large Language Models
related_work.relevance: Tests robustness to adversarial perturbations (typos, synonym swaps, etc.). We focus on natural paraphrases and format changes, measuring consistency rather than adversarial robustness.

related_work.title: Justice or Prejudice? Quantifying Biases in LLM-as-a-Judge
related_work.authors: Ye, Wang, Huang, Chen, et al.
related_work.relevance: Quantifies biases in LLM-as-judge paradigm. Informs our design of the answer equivalence evaluation component.

hypothesis: LLMs exhibit significant cross-format inconsistency (Cross-Format Agreement < 85% for most models), with different paraphrase types causing non-uniform consistency degradation (negation-framing and syntactic restructuring causing >2x larger drops than lexical substitution). Consistency varies significantly across knowledge domains and is not fully predicted by accuracy alone, providing unique diagnostic value beyond standard benchmarks. The 70B model shows higher consistency than 7-8B models, but the gap between accuracy and consistency remains substantial even at scale.
success_criteria: Cross-Format Agreement (CFA) is below 90% for the majority of tested models, confirming that format choice meaningfully affects model answers
Different paraphrase types produce statistically significantly different Paraphrase Fragility Index (PFI) scores, enabling actionable diagnosis of model weaknesses

Consistency metrics provide information beyond accuracy: models with similar accuracy show meaningfully different consistency profiles (Consistency-Accuracy Ratio varies by >10% among accuracy-matched models)
Domain-stratified analysis reveals significant variation in consistency across knowledge domains (>10 percentage point spread in Domain Consistency Score)
Size scaling analysis shows meaningful consistency improvement from 7-8B to 70B but with remaining gaps even at 70B scale

feasibility.compute: All experiments are inference-only on a single A6000 GPU. Open-weight models (3.8-14B) fit in <30GB; Llama-3.1-70B with 4-bit AWQ fits in ~40GB. Estimated total inference time: 4-5 hours for ~8,000-10,000 instances across 6-7 models.
feasibility.data: Base questions sourced from openly available QA datasets (MMLU, ARC, GSM8K, CommonsenseQA, TriviaQA, LogiQA). No proprietary data required.
feasibility.time: Dataset construction ~2 hours, model evaluation ~5 hours, analysis ~1 hour. Total: ~8 hours within budget.
feasibility.risk_mitigation: If 70B quantized model is too slow, we allocate it a subset of questions. All other models (3.8-14B) are fast on A6000. LLM-based paraphrase generation and answer equivalence judging are batched efficiently.

contributions: First benchmark evaluating item-level cross-format consistency across 5 formats (MCQ, open-ended, yes/no, true/false, fill-in-the-blank), going beyond OSQ-bench's aggregate accuracy gap
First paraphrase typology analysis for LLM consistency, decomposing effects by 6 paraphrase types (lexical, syntactic, voice, formality, negation, elaborative)
Novel consistency metrics (CFA, CPA, FCAG, CAR, PFI, DCS) that decouple consistency from accuracy
Domain-stratified consistency profiling across 6 knowledge domains with difficulty stratification
Size-scaling analysis from 3.8B to 70B showing how consistency evolves with scale
Two-stage answer equivalence evaluation (rule-based + LLM judge) validated against human annotations
